epigraph.text: In all your ways acknowledge him, and he will make straight your paths.
epigraph.quote: The art of integration lies not in calculating but in choosing the right path.

content.text:
Before defining complex integrals, we must make precise what we mean by 
a path or contour in the complex plane.

content.title: Parametrized Curve
content.content:
A **parametrized curve** (or **path**) 
is a continuous function $\gamma: [a, b] \to \C$. We write 
$\gamma(t) = x(t) + iy(t)$ where $x, y: [a, b] \to \R$ are continuous.

The point $\gamma(a)$ is the **initial point** and $\gamma(b)$ is 
the **terminal point**. If $\gamma(a) = \gamma(b)$, the curve is 
**closed**.

content.title: Common contours
content.problem:
1. **Line segment** from $z_1$ to $z_2$: 
 $\gamma(t) = (1-t)z_1 + tz_2$, $t \in [0, 1]$.

2. **Circle** of radius $R$ centered at $z_0$: 
 $\gamma(t) = z_0 + Re^{it}$, $t \in [0, 2\pi]$.

3. **Upper semicircle**: 
 $\gamma(t) = Re^{it}$, $t \in [0, \pi]$.

content.title: Smooth Curve
content.content:
A curve $\gamma: [a, b] \to \C$ is **smooth** if 
$\gamma'(t)$ exists, is continuous on $[a, b]$, and $\gamma'(t) \neq 0$ 
for all $t \in [a, b]$.

content.text:
The condition $\gamma'(t) \neq 0$ ensures the curve has a well-defined 
tangent direction at each point.

content.title: Contour
content.content:
A **contour** (or **piecewise smooth curve**) is 
a curve that can be written as a finite union of smooth curves joined 
end-to-end:
\[
 \gamma = \gamma_1 + \gamma_2 + \cdots + \gamma_n,
\]
where the terminal point of $\gamma_k$ equals the initial point of 
$\gamma_{k+1}$.

content.title: Opposite Contour
content.content:
The **opposite contour** $-\gamma$ traverses 
the same points as $\gamma$ but in the reverse direction:
\[
 (-\gamma)(t) = \gamma(a + b - t), \quad t \in [a, b].
\]

content.title: Simple Curve
content.content:
A curve $\gamma$ is **simple** (or 
**Jordan curve**) if it does not cross itself—that is, 
$\gamma(t_1) = \gamma(t_2)$ with $t_1 < t_2$ implies $t_1 = a$ and $t_2 = b$ 
(so the only possible self-intersection is the endpoints for a closed curve).

content.title: Jordan Curve Theorem
content.content:
A simple closed curve $\gamma$ divides the plane into exactly two 
connected regions: a bounded **interior** and an unbounded 
**exterior**. The curve is the common boundary of both.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This theorem is intuitively obvious but notoriously difficult to prove 
rigorously. We take it as given.

marginNotes.text:
For everything there is a season, and 
a time for every matter under heaven.

marginNotes.author: Henri Poincaré
marginNotes.text:
It is by logic that we prove, but by 
intuition that we discover.
